cataloging_subject.label: Prosalegenden
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Carl Horstmann's 1880 editorial selection as represented in CME00061: German introductions and apparatus framing Caxton's Life and Translation of Saint Winifred, a John Mirk Winifred sermon, fifteen Marian miracles, a Life of Saint Dorothy, and a nineteen-chapter Life and Miracles of Saint Jerome. The record does not collapse those separately transmitted medieval works, their physical witnesses and early-print copies, the Anglia article, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one medieval compilation.

catalog_summary.title: Prosalegenden
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous and composite medieval authorship; William Caxton and John Mirk have scoped responsibilities
catalog_summary.date: Medieval components chiefly attested in the fifteenth century; Caxton print 1484; editorial selection published 1880
catalog_summary.region: England and Welsh saint traditions; edited and published at Halle an der Saale, Germany

notes: An editorial article title, not a title for one medieval work or medieval compilation.

titles.value: Caxton's Ausgabe der Legende von S. Wenefreda
Here begynneth the lyf of the holy & blessid vyrgyn saynt Wenefryde

agents.name: Carl Horstmann
notes: Editor, transcriber, collator, compiler, and German commentator of the 1880 selection.

agents.name: Anonymous authors and compilers of the MS 432 hagiographic texts
notes: A modeling collective, not an assertion that all three components share one author.

agents.name: Fuller
notes: The Dorothy and Jerome materials include quod Fuller statements; the consulted sources do not securely identify the person's full name or exact role.

scope.description: Assembly of independently transmitted items into the article Prosalegenden
Middle English translation and abridgment represented by the 1484 print

assertion.notes: Current cataloging calls the English text an abridged version of Robert of Shrewsbury; the exact exemplar and degree of compilation remain unsettled.

scope.description: Authorship of the Latin source work represented only through Caxton's abridged Middle English version

assertion.notes: This does not identify Caxton's physical exemplar.
Anonymous responsibility is scoped collectively for browsing and does not imply common authorship.
The repeated quod Fuller may signal a scribe, owner, compiler, or another role; no stronger attribution is made.

date_statements.display: Published as Carl Horstmann's Prosalegenden in Anglia 3 (1880)
Printed by William Caxton at Westminster in 1484; the translation and abridgment existed by that date
Composed by the early fifteenth century; the direct Cotton witness is dated 1425-1449

assertion.notes: The normalized range is deliberately broad and does not equate witness date with exact composition.

date_statements.display: The selected Marian, Dorothy, and Jerome texts are attested in fifteenth-century Lambeth MS 432
notes: This dates the witness, not necessarily the composition of each underlying legend.

scope.description: Publication place of Anglia 3 and the editorial collection

notes: This is the origin assigned to the Cotton witness, not a precise place of composition for the sermon.

scope.description: Middle English component traditions and witnesses
Horstmann's introductions, source notes, and footnote apparatus
Caxton office, prayers, rubrics, quoted sources, and manuscript devotional supplements

form_statements.description: All principal cataloged medieval items are prose. Two encoded Latin verse lines in one LG occur within embedded devotional apparatus and do not make the edited selection mixed-form.

subject_statements.label: Saint Winifred: virginity, martyrdom, restoration, and cult
Translation of relics and shrine devotion
Pilgrimage, holy wells, shrines, and miraculous healing
Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion
Saint Jerome, visions, purgatory, hell, and posthumous miracles
Transcription, abbreviation expansion, and witness collation

summaries.value: A hagiographic selection centered on saints, miracles, and devotional practice. Caxton's Winifred narrative recounts the Welsh virgin's attempted sexual coercion, decapitation, miraculous restoration, religious life, death, and the translation of her relics to Shrewsbury; Mirk retells the life and shrine miracles as a festal sermon. Fifteen Marian exempla emphasize prayer and intercession. Dorothy's life narrates virgin martyrdom and the winter roses and apples sent to Theophilus. The Jerome cycle combines a life, pseudo-epistolary visions, descriptions of the afterlife, and posthumous miracles.

The XML has six top-level divisions: Horstmann's Winifred preface; the Caxton section with Life, Translation, and Mirk addendum; a bridging commentary; Marian legends; Dorothy; and Jerome. Jerome contains an introduction and nineteen numbered chapters. The source pages 314-316 contain two physically simultaneous streams: the lower continuation of Caxton's Latin office and the upper beginning of Mirk's sermon.

The title Prosalegenden and the four Roman-numbered groupings belong to Horstmann's 1880 article. Dates, places, authorship, and source witnesses are therefore scoped to components. The collection is cataloged as prose despite a two-line Latin verse group and verse-like prayers embedded in apparatus. Horstmann's isolated MS 402 and Marian fol. 95 statements conflict with exact MS 432 content evidence and are retained as errors/open questions.

extent.description: Four Roman-numbered hagiographic groupings inside six DIV2 elements, with 23 nested DIV3 parts (29 structural divisions total). The XML also contains 104 prose paragraphs, one two-line verse group, 71 page-break elements, 560 milestones, and 126 notes.

content_parts.label: Horstmann's introduction to Caxton's Winifred
content_parts.summary: German discussion of Winifred's cult, Mirk's Festial, possible Latin sources, surviving Caxton copies, and transcription practice.

content_parts.label: Caxton's Life and Translation of Saint Winifred
content_parts.summary: The 1484 Caxton prose life, relic translation, Latin office, and Horstmann's appended Mirk sermon.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Winifred
content_parts.summary: Winifred's vow of virginity, decapitation by Cradoc, restoration, holy well, religious life, and death.

locators.value: opening Life section; exact signature mapping retained in full facsimile

content_parts.label: Translation of Saint Winifred and Latin office
content_parts.summary: The transfer of Winifred's relics to Shrewsbury, shrine miracles, Caxton's explicit, and associated Latin liturgical material.
notes: Its lower-page Latin office overlaps Mirk's upper-page addendum on printed pp. 314-316.

content_parts.label: John Mirk's sermon for Saint Winifred
content_parts.summary: A Festial sermon retelling Winifred's life, translation to Shrewsbury, and miracles for a preaching audience.

locators.value: begins f. 80v; exact terminal folio unresolved

content_parts.label: Horstmann's introduction to the MS 432 texts
content_parts.summary: German source description and outline of the Jerome cycle, with an erroneous reference to MS 402.

content_parts.label: Fifteen Marian legends
content_parts.summary: Fifteen brief miracles centered on Ave Maria devotion, Marian aid, rescue, healing, intercession, and visionary reward.

locators.value: starts f. 85; inferred through f. 89v before Dorothy at f. 90

notes: Horstmann's heading says f. 95, conflicting with Lambeth's current f. 85 content record.

content_parts.label: Life of Saint Dorothy
content_parts.summary: Dorothy's virginity, tortures, martyrdom, winter roses and apples, Theophilus's conversion, and a Latin prayer ending quod Fuller.

content_parts.label: Life and Miracles of Saint Jerome
content_parts.summary: An introduction and nineteen chapters drawing on the Golden Legend, pseudo-Augustinian and pseudo-Cyrillian letters, visions of the afterlife, miracles, and Birgittine revelations.

related_works.title: The life of the holy and blessed virgin Saint Winifred; Translation of Saint Winifred
Festial
notes: CME00061 contains only Mirk's Saint Winifred sermon, not the Festial as a whole.

related_works.title: Vita Sanctae Wenefredae
notes: The relation applies to Caxton's scoped component and does not identify a physical source manuscript.

related_works.title: Legenda aurea
notes: Named as a source within the Jerome cycle and in Horstmann's discussion, not modeled as a single physical exemplar.

evidence.summary: Primary evidence for titles, contents, languages, prose form, subjects, responsibilities, section structure, incipits, editorial notes, and XML representation.
Supports division, paragraph, verse, note, milestone, page-break, and italic-markup counts, including repeated PB 314-316.

evidence.citation: BSB complete scan and visual audit of Carl Horstmann, Prosalegenden (1880).
evidence.summary: Supports the editorial-unit boundary, publication facts, source headings, section pages, German commentary, subjects, source statements, and page-layout interpretation.

evidence.citation: Lambeth MS 306 catalog and complete facsimile audit.
evidence.summary: Supports the Caxton component's physical locus, incomplete state, reversed leaves, exact canvases, and distinction between the print and manuscript codex.
Supports witness date, physical description, Jerome, Marian, and Dorothy loci, component titles, and the correction from MS 402 to MS 432.

evidence.citation: British Library Cotton Claudius A II records.
evidence.summary: Supports Mirk authorship, Festial context, witness date, West Midlands origin, and whole-codex extent.

evidence.citation: James catalog and modern bibliography for Caius MS 168/89.
evidence.summary: Supports the current/historical identifiers, fifteenth-century paper Festial description, and relationship to Cotton Claudius A II.

evidence.citation: ISTC iw00062000 and current Morgan copy records.
evidence.summary: Supports Caxton title, translation, 1484 Westminster imprint, ideal extent, identifiers, source attribution, and surviving-copy context.

open_questions.question: Should future viewer records expose the five medieval textual components as separately selectable child works while retaining Prosalegenden as the encoded collection record?
next_steps: Establish stable child-work identifiers without duplicating the CME00061 PDF representation.
Retain component-scoped authorship, date, form, and lineage bindings.

open_questions.question: Can direct inspection reconcile Horstmann's MS 402 and f. 95 statements with the current MS 432 and f. 85 cataloging?
next_steps: Inspect historic Lambeth catalogs and physical foliation before changing current scoped loci.

open_questions.question: Who was Fuller, and does quod Fuller identify a scribe, compiler, translator, owner, or another participant in MS 432?
next_steps: Inspect the complete manuscript and current codicological scholarship.
Do not convert the name into an author attribution without supporting evidence.

open_questions.question: What exact Latin exemplar or source combination underlies Caxton's Winifred text?
next_steps: Compare the complete Caxton text with Robert of Shrewsbury's manuscript tradition and current scholarship.
Keep the translation_of relation at abstract-work level until a physical exemplar is evidenced.

notes: The repository viewer should present Prosalegenden as an edited selection and expose the component labels and scoped lineages rather than showing one undifferentiated author/date.

Downloaded source presence is recorded in the lineage manifest. BSB Anglia 3 and Lambeth MS 306 caches are complete whole-provider objects; no public complete surrogate was found for the other three manuscript witnesses.
Italic letters in CME00061 chiefly encode Horstmann's expansion/supply typography, not photographed manuscript or incunable abbreviation shapes.
